comparePage.title: AACsearch vs The Alternatives
comparePage.description: See how AACsearch compares to Algolia, Elasticsearch, and MeiliSearch — and why thousands of developers choose us.

cta.title: Ready to switch?
cta.description: Migration from Algolia or Elasticsearch takes less than a day with our connector API.

compare.title: The honest comparison
compare.subtitle: We are not the right choice for every team. Here is a frank breakdown of where AACsearch wins and where it does not.

vsAlgolia.title: AACsearch vs Algolia
vsAlgolia.description: Algolia is polished and has excellent docs. AACsearch is more affordable and built for multi-tenant teams. If you are spending $500+/mo on Algolia, AACsearch will cut that significantly.

vsElasticsearch.title: AACsearch vs Elasticsearch
vsElasticsearch.description: Elasticsearch is infinitely flexible and infinitely complex. AACsearch is opinionated and simple. If you need a SIEM or log analytics, use Elastic. If you need product search, use us.

vsMeilisearch.title: AACsearch vs Meilisearch
vsMeilisearch.description: Meilisearch is an excellent open-source engine. AACsearch adds the hosted layer: scoped tokens, multi-tenancy, analytics, and a control plane.

vsTypesense.title: AACsearch vs Typesense Cloud
vsTypesense.description: AACsearch is built on Typesense. We add multi-tenancy, API key management, scoped tokens, and a SaaS control plane on top of the engine.

vsCustom.title: AACsearch vs DIY
vsCustom.description: Building your own hosted search means managing nodes, upgrades, backups, and monitoring. AACsearch does that so you can ship features.

whenNotUs.title: When to use something else
whenNotUs.description: Log analytics → Elasticsearch. Full-text enterprise search with compliance → Coveo. Simple autocomplete on 1K records → don't bother with any service.

compareAlgoliaPage.title: AACsearch vs Algolia: 5x Cheaper Search with Zero Lock-In (2026)
compareAlgoliaPage.description: Detailed AACsearch vs Algolia comparison. AACsearch delivers the same search quality at 1/5th the cost with instant deployment, built-in analytics, Typesense-powered relevance, and no vendor lock-in.

compareAlgolia.title: AACsearch vs Algolia — The Honest Comparison
compareAlgolia.subtitle: Algolia is the market leader with excellent DX. AACsearch is more affordable and adds multi-tenant isolation, a search widget, and CMS connectors — all included.

pricing.title: Pricing
pricing.description: Algolia charges per search operation and per record stored, with minimums. AACsearch charges flat per-index pricing with unlimited operations. At 100k docs + 500k searches/mo: Algolia ~$499/mo, AACsearch $99/mo.

performance.title: Performance
performance.description: AACsearch is powered by Typesense — the fastest open-source search engine with sub-10ms query latency. Algolia offers comparable speed but costs significantly more per query.

multiTenancy.title: Multi-tenancy
multiTenancy.description: Algolia uses per-index API keys with user tokens. AACsearch uses HMAC scoped tokens with organization-level isolation — built-in, no middleware needed.

security.title: Security & Lock-in
security.description: Algolia uses a proprietary backend — your data is locked in. AACsearch is powered by Typesense (open-source). Export your data anytime and self-host with zero migration cost.

migration.title: Migration
migration.description: The AACsearch REST API is structurally similar to Algolia's. A migration script can export your Algolia data and import it into AACsearch in minutes.

verdict.title: When to choose AACsearch
verdict.description: If you are spending $200+/mo on Algolia, or if multi-tenancy isolation and predictable pricing matter for your business, AACsearch is a clear upgrade.

compareAlgolia.codeSubtitle: The AACsearch search API mirrors Algolia's. Swap the client, keep the filters.

table.title: Feature Comparison
table.subtitle: A side-by-side look at what each platform offers

scenario1.algolia: Algolia: Free tier (10k ops + 10k records) — hits record limit, needs Growth at $299/mo
scenario1.aacsearch: AACsearch: Free tier (1 index, 10k docs) fits perfectly — $0/mo

whyMigrate.title: Why Teams Migrate to AACsearch
items: Pricing shock — Algolia's per-operation billing becomes unpredictable as traffic grows
No vendor lock-in — AACsearch is built on Typesense. Your data is portable.
All-in-one — Search + analytics + widget + connectors in one product, not 3 add-ons
Self-serve migration — Import your Algolia data directly, reindex in minutes

testimonial.quote: We were paying Algolia $499/mo for a basic e-commerce search. Migrated to AACsearch in an afternoon — same relevance, 5x cheaper, and the built-in analytics showed us exactly what customers were searching for but not finding.
testimonial.author: Founder, mid-market e-commerce company

whenAACsearch.heading: Choose AACsearch if you:
items: Want search-as-a-service with zero DevOps
Need multi-tenant search for your SaaS product
Want analytics, widget, and connectors without building them
Are paying $200+/mo on Algolia and want predictable pricing
Value Typesense portability — export and self-host anytime

whenAlgolia.heading: Choose Algolia if you:
items: Rely on Algolia-specific features (AI recommendations, personalization)
Need enterprise compliance certifications (SOC 2, HIPAA via Enterprise plan)
Have an existing Algolia integration with minimal search volume
Need advanced Algolia APIs (AB testing, dynamic re-ranking)

cta.heading: Ready to switch?
cta.description: Start a free trial — no credit card required. Import your Algolia data in minutes.

compareElasticsearchPage.title: AACsearch vs Elasticsearch: The Honest Alternative (2026)
compareElasticsearchPage.description: Detailed AACsearch vs Elasticsearch comparison. AACsearch delivers zero-ops search, automatic scaling, built-in analytics, and predictable flat pricing — without Elasticsearch cluster management overhead. The true elasticsearch alternative for modern applications.

compareElasticsearch.title: AACsearch vs Elasticsearch — The Honest Comparison
compareElasticsearch.subtitle: Elasticsearch is a powerful general-purpose data platform. AACsearch is a purpose-built search API. Different tools for different jobs — here is when each makes sense.

complexity.title: Operational complexity
complexity.description: Elasticsearch requires cluster management, JVM tuning, shard optimization, and ongoing maintenance. AACsearch is a hosted API — zero-ops, no infrastructure to manage.

latency.title: Search latency
latency.description: Elasticsearch p99 search latency is typically 100-400ms depending on cluster size and query complexity. AACsearch targets under 50ms p99 for product and document search workloads.

useCase.title: Use case fit
useCase.description: Elasticsearch excels at log analytics, observability pipelines, full-text enterprise search, and complex aggregations. AACsearch excels at product search, help center search, and SaaS application search.

cost.title: Cost
cost.description: Elasticsearch Cloud is expensive for pure search workloads — you pay for cluster nodes you don't need. AACsearch pricing is based on search-units — pay only for what you use.

schema.title: Schema management
schema.description: Elasticsearch mapping changes are complex and often require reindexing with downtime. AACsearch has zero-downtime alias-swap reindex — schema changes without any interruption.

verdict.title: When to choose AACsearch
verdict.description: If your use case is product search, help center, or SaaS full-text search — and you do not need log analytics or complex aggregations — AACsearch is the simpler, cheaper, faster option.

compareElasticsearch.codeSubtitle: AACsearch is a managed search API. Elasticsearch requires cluster provisioning, JVM tuning, and ongoing maintenance.

table.title: Feature Comparison
table.subtitle: A side-by-side look at how AACsearch and Elasticsearch compare across key dimensions

pricingModel.title: Pricing model
pricingModel.elasticsearch: Per node-hour + storage (cloud) or self-hosted infra cost

multiTenancy.title: Multi-tenancy
multiTenancy.elasticsearch: Requires separate indices or cross-cluster search setup

widget.title: Widget (embeddable UI)
widget.elasticsearch: Requires custom UI with Elasticsearch JS client

scopedTokens.title: Scoped API tokens
scopedTokens.elasticsearch: Requires API key setup with role-based access control

logRetention.title: Log retention
logRetention.elasticsearch: Configurable (self-managed) / varies by Elastic Cloud plan

scenario1.heading: Small e-commerce (10k products, 50k searches/mo)
scenario1.elasticsearch: Elasticsearch: Self-hosted (free) or Elastic Cloud ~$95/mo (2-node cluster)
scenario1.aacsearch: AACsearch: Free tier (1 index, 10k docs) fits perfectly — $0/mo
scenario1.saving: Saving: Up to $95/mo vs Elastic Cloud

scenario2.heading: Mid-market (100k products, 500k searches/mo)
scenario2.elasticsearch: Elasticsearch: Elastic Cloud ~$350-600/mo (3-node, 120GB) + DevOps time for cluster management
scenario2.aacsearch: AACsearch: Scale plan $99/mo — zero ops, automatic scaling

scenario3.heading: Enterprise (1M products, 5M searches/mo)
scenario3.elasticsearch: Elasticsearch: Elastic Cloud ~$1,200-2,500/mo (5-10 nodes) + dedicated SRE team
scenario3.saving: Saving: ~$700-2,000/mo plus full SRE cost elimination

whyMigrate.title: Why Teams Migrate from Elasticsearch to AACsearch
items: Zero-ops — No cluster provisioning, JVM tuning, or shard management. AACsearch is fully managed.
Automatic scaling — AACsearch handles traffic spikes without capacity planning or cluster resizing.
Predictable pricing — Flat per-index cost vs unpredictable node-hour + storage bills from Elastic Cloud.
Built-in analytics — Search analytics out of the box. No Kibana setup, no dashboard maintenance.

testimonial.quote: We spent more time managing our Elasticsearch cluster than actually building search features. Migrated to AACsearch in a weekend — zero ops, lower latency, and the dashboard showed us search gaps we had no visibility into before.
testimonial.author: CTO, B2B SaaS company (migrated from Elasticsearch Cloud)

whenAACsearch.heading: Choose AACsearch if you:
items: Want zero-ops search — no cluster provisioning or JVM tuning
Need automatic scaling without capacity planning
Want search analytics, widget, and connectors included
Are paying Elastic Cloud bills and want predictable pricing
Build product search, help center, or SaaS application search

whenElasticsearch.heading: Choose Elasticsearch if you:
items: Need log analytics / observability pipelines (ELK stack)
Require complex aggregations and data analytics queries
Need on-prem control with full self-hosting flexibility
Have an existing Elasticsearch infrastructure with committed investment
Require advanced ES features (machine learning, graph exploration)

cta.heading: Ready to switch from Elasticsearch?
cta.description: Start a free trial — no credit card required. Zero-ops search in minutes, not weeks.

compareMeilisearchPage.title: AACsearch vs Meilisearch
compareMeilisearchPage.description: AACsearch vs Meilisearch — engine comparison, hosted layer, multi-tenancy, and when to pick each.

compareMeilisearch.title: AACsearch vs Meilisearch
compareMeilisearch.subtitle: Meilisearch is an excellent open-source engine. AACsearch adds the hosted layer your production deployment needs.

engine.title: Underlying engine
engine.description: Meilisearch is the engine and the product. AACsearch uses a different open-source engine with vector search built in.

hosted.title: Managed cloud
hosted.description: Meilisearch Cloud is their hosted option. AACsearch's hosted layer adds scoped tokens, multi-tenancy, and a management control plane.

multiTenancy.title: Multi-tenancy
multiTenancy.description: Meilisearch's multi-tenancy uses tenant tokens. AACsearch's scoped tokens carry AND-combined mandatory filters for stronger isolation.

analytics.title: Analytics
analytics.description: Meilisearch has basic search analytics. AACsearch tracks every search event with full metadata: query, filters, latency, CTR.

connectors.title: CMS connectors
connectors.description: Meilisearch has a JavaScript SDK-first approach. AACsearch has native PHP modules for PrestaShop and 1C-Bitrix.

verdict.title: When to choose AACsearch
verdict.description: If you need multi-tenant isolation, a management API, CMS connectors, or analytics beyond basic query counts.

compareTypesensePage.title: AACsearch vs Typesense Cloud: Better Alternative with Zero Lock-In (2026)
compareTypesensePage.description: Detailed AACsearch vs Typesense Cloud comparison. AACsearch adds managed multi-tenancy, analytics, search widget, and connectors on top of Typesense — all included with per-index pricing and zero lock-in. The true Typesense Cloud alternative.

compareTypesense.title: Engine vs. managed cloud platform
compareTypesense.subtitle: Typesense is the engine we run. AACsearch wraps it with the SaaS layer your team needs to go from zero to production in an afternoon.

hosting.title: No ops overhead
hosting.description: AACsearch manages Typesense upgrades, cluster sizing, backup, and monitoring. You search — we run the infrastructure.

multiTenancy.title: Multi-tenancy built in
multiTenancy.description: Org-scoped API keys, scoped tokens, and per-key origin allow-lists are AACsearch additions. Raw Typesense has no multi-tenant layer.

analytics.title: Search analytics
analytics.description: AACsearch adds a full analytics pipeline: query logs, zero-result tracking, top queries, and a dashboard. Not included in raw Typesense.

connectors.title: CMS connectors
connectors.description: PrestaShop, Bitrix, WordPress, and Shopify connectors are AACsearch additions. Typesense has an API — connectors are built separately.

pricing.title: Predictable pricing
pricing.description: AACsearch pricing is per search-unit — you know your bill before the month ends. Typesense self-host means variable infrastructure costs.

support.title: Dedicated support
support.description: AACsearch plans include email support and an SLA. Typesense community support is forum-based with no response time guarantees.

compareTypesense.codeSubtitle: AACsearch adds multi-tenancy, analytics, and managed infrastructure on top of the same Typesense engine you already trust.

table.title: Feature Comparison
table.subtitle: A side-by-side look at what each platform offers

scenario1.heading: Launch (10k docs, basic search)
scenario1.typesenseCloud: Typesense Cloud: $49/mo (1-node, no analytics, no multi-tenant)
scenario1.aacsearch: AACsearch: Free tier (1 index, 10k docs) — all features included

scenario2.heading: Growth (100k docs, multi-tenant)
scenario2.aacsearch: AACsearch: Scale plan $99/mo — all built-in, no custom dev

scenario3.heading: Scale (1M docs, analytics + connectors)
scenario3.aacsearch: AACsearch: Pro plan $499/mo — all built-in, zero dev

whyMigrate.title: Why Teams Choose AACsearch Over Typesense Cloud
items: Full product, not just infrastructure — AACsearch is search-as-a-service with API keys, multi-tenancy, analytics, dashboard, widget, and connectors out of the box
Multi-tenancy without the boilerplate — Each organization gets isolated search collections with scoped API tokens. Typesense Cloud requires you to build your own tenant isolation layer.
No middleware to build — Rate limiting, user management, webhook notifications, and API key management are all included. On Typesense Cloud you build everything yourself.
Predictable per-index pricing — Flat rate with unlimited operations. No surprise bills from node-hour + per-GB storage calculations.

testimonial.quote: We started with Typesense Cloud but quickly realized we were spending more time building infrastructure around it than shipping search features. AACsearch gave us everything — multi-tenancy, analytics, the widget — without the middleware tax.
testimonial.author: Engineering Lead, B2B SaaS platform

whenAACsearch.heading: Choose AACsearch if you:
items: Want search-as-a-service — not just a database cluster
Need multi-tenant search for your SaaS product
Want analytics, widget, and connectors without building them
Are comparing Typesense pricing and want per-index predictability
Value Typesense portability — export and self-host anytime

whenTypesenseCloud.heading: Choose Typesense Cloud if you:
items: Need raw Typesense API access and plan to build your own infrastructure
Already have a custom multi-tenancy solution in place
Don't need analytics, widgets, or CMS connectors
Prefer to manage infrastructure directly with full cluster control

analogy.heading: The Simple Difference
analogy.line1: Typesense Cloud is like renting a server from AWS.
analogy.line2: AACsearch is like Heroku for search — you get the platform, the tooling, and the dashboard.

cta.heading: Ready to switch?
cta.description: Start a free trial — no credit card required. Powered by Typesense, no lock-in, export anytime.

compareOpenSearchPage.title: AACsearch vs OpenSearch
compareOpenSearchPage.description: AACsearch is a focused, fast search API. OpenSearch is a full analytics platform. Here is when each makes sense.

compareOpenSearch.title: Focused search vs. analytics platform
compareOpenSearch.subtitle: OpenSearch is powerful but complex. AACsearch is purpose-built for application search — simpler schema, faster setup, lower latency.

complexity.title: Days, not weeks
complexity.description: AACsearch indexes your first collection in an afternoon. OpenSearch cluster setup, mapping design, and ILM policies take a team and weeks.

performance.title: Sub-50ms vs. 100-500ms
performance.description: AACsearch P99 latency is under 50ms. OpenSearch query latency typically ranges 100-500ms on equivalent hardware.

schema.title: Schema simplicity
schema.description: AACsearch collections have a flat, typed schema. OpenSearch mapping design with nested objects and dynamic templates is a specialty skill.

multiTenancy.title: Native multi-tenancy
multiTenancy.description: AACsearch org-scoped keys and scoped tokens are first-class features. OpenSearch multi-tenancy requires index-per-tenant or security plugins.

analytics.title: Search-specific analytics
analytics.description: AACsearch provides query logs, zero-result rates, and CTR — built for product teams. OpenSearch analytics targets infrastructure monitoring.

pricing.title: Predictable cost
pricing.description: AACsearch charges per search-unit. OpenSearch on AWS costs depend on instance type, storage I/O, and data transfer — difficult to forecast.

cost.title: Cost comparison
cost.description: OpenSearch self-hosted requires dedicated infrastructure, engineering time, and ongoing maintenance. AACsearch is fully managed with predictable per-unit pricing.

relevance.title: Search relevance
relevance.description: AACsearch uses BM25 scoring with configurable field weights, boost rules, and typo tolerance. OpenSearch requires manual tuning of similarity configurations.

setup.title: Setup time
setup.description: AACsearch is API-ready in minutes. OpenSearch self-hosted takes days to provision, configure, and secure before a single document can be indexed.

speed.title: Query speed
speed.description: AACsearch delivers sub-50ms P99 query latency. OpenSearch latency varies widely with index size, shard count, and cluster load.

compareSolrPage.title: AACsearch vs Apache Solr
compareSolrPage.description: Solr is battle-tested but requires specialists. AACsearch gives you the same reliability with a modern API and zero operations overhead.

compareSolr.title: Modern API vs. legacy complexity
compareSolr.subtitle: Solr has powered enterprise search for 20 years. AACsearch gives a junior developer the same capabilities with a one-afternoon setup.

modernApi.title: REST API vs. Solr query DSL
modernApi.description: AACsearch uses a clean JSON REST API. Solr queries use its own DSL — q, fq, fl, facet.field — with a steep learning curve.

setup.title: Minutes vs. days
setup.description: AACsearch setup: create an index, ingest documents, call the API. Solr requires Java, ZooKeeper (for SolrCloud), schema design, and core management.

performance.title: Faster out of the box
performance.description: AACsearch returns results in under 50ms with no tuning. Solr performance requires expert configuration: cache sizing and JVM tuning.

multiTenancy.title: Built-in multi-tenancy
multiTenancy.description: AACsearch isolates tenants at the API key level. Solr multi-tenancy means separate cores or collections with manual access control.

analyticsBuiltin.title: Built-in analytics
analyticsBuiltin.description: AACsearch records every query and provides a dashboard. Solr has no built-in query analytics — you need to parse logs with external tools.

pricing.title: No infrastructure cost
pricing.description: AACsearch is fully managed. Solr means provisioning, operating, and scaling your own cluster — or paying for an expensive managed service.

devex.title: Developer experience
devex.description: AACsearch offers a modern REST API with typed SDKs, OpenAPI spec, and a dashboard. Solrs XML configuration and Java-centric tooling have a steep learning curve.

migration.title: Migration path
migration.description: Migrate from Solr to AACsearch via the REST API. Reindex documents, configure schema through the dashboard, and point your application to the AACsearch endpoint.

ops.title: Operations
ops.description: AACsearch manages infrastructure, scaling, upgrades, and monitoring. Solr self-hosted requires dedicated ops teams for ZooKeeper, shard management, and cluster tuning.

search.title: Search quality
search.description: AACsearch provides typo tolerance, synonyms, faceted search, relevance tuning, and analytics out of the box. Solr requires plugin configuration and custom development for equivalent features.

compareWeaviatePage.title: AACsearch vs Weaviate
compareWeaviatePage.description: Weaviate is a vector database. AACsearch is a full-text plus vector hybrid search API. Here is what you get from each.

compareWeaviate.title: Vector DB vs. hybrid search API
compareWeaviate.subtitle: Both support vectors, but AACsearch adds keyword search, facets, typo tolerance, and a multi-tenant hosting layer that Weaviate lacks.

hybridSearch.title: Keyword + vector in one call
hybridSearch.description: AACsearch hybrid search combines BM25 keyword relevance with vector similarity in one query. Weaviate BM25 support is secondary to its vector focus.

facets.title: Faceted navigation
facets.description: AACsearch computes facet counts on every query — essential for e-commerce and documentation search. Weaviate has no native faceted navigation.

typoTolerance.title: Typo tolerance
typoTolerance.description: AACsearch has built-in fuzzy matching and typo tolerance for end-user queries. Weaviate vector search does not help with misspelled terms.

multiTenancy.title: Native multi-tenancy
multiTenancy.description: AACsearch org-scoped keys and per-tenant analytics are first-class features. Weaviate multi-tenancy requires separate schemas and manual isolation.

pricing.title: Transparent pricing
pricing.description: AACsearch charges per search-unit with a predictable monthly bill. Weaviate cloud pricing is dimension times vector times query — complex to forecast.

widget.title: Drop-in search widget
widget.description: AACsearch ships a 14KB browser widget with instant search, facets, and analytics. Weaviate has no equivalent — you build the UI from scratch.

features.title: Feature depth
features.description: AACsearch offers full-text search, vector search, hybrid search, typo tolerance, synonyms, faceted navigation, and analytics. Weaviate focuses primarily on vector search with AI-powered features.

schema.title: Schema management
schema.description: AACsearch schema management is straightforward via dashboard or API. Weaviate follows a class-property schema model that requires understanding of vectorization and module configuration.

support.title: Support and SLAs
support.description: AACsearch offers email support across paid plans and SLAs on enterprise tier. Weaviate offers community support for open-source and enterprise support for Weaviate Cloud.

usecase.title: Use case fit
usecase.description: AACsearch excels at product search, site search, and knowledge base search. Weaviate is strongest for AI-native applications, knowledge graphs, and semantic search at scale.

comparePineconePage.title: AACsearch vs Pinecone
comparePineconePage.description: Pinecone is a pure vector database. AACsearch combines keyword and vector search for applications where both relevance modes matter.

comparePinecone.title: Pure vector vs. full search platform
comparePinecone.subtitle: Pinecone excels at pure vector recall. AACsearch adds the keyword search, facets, and product UX layer that most applications actually need.

keywordSearch.title: Keyword + semantic
keywordSearch.description: AACsearch handles both typo-tolerant keyword search and semantic vector search. Pinecone is vector-only — keyword queries need a separate system.

facets.title: Faceted navigation
facets.description: AACsearch computes facet counts with every query. Pinecone metadata filtering is available but with no facet count computation.

hybridSearch.title: Configurable hybrid weights
hybridSearch.description: AACsearch lets you tune the balance between BM25 and vector similarity per query. Pinecone hybrid search has limited weighting control.

pricing.title: Simpler pricing
pricing.description: AACsearch charges per search-unit. Pinecone charges per vector dimension stored times queries times pod size — complex to model.

performance.title: Search API, not just a vector store
performance.description: AACsearch returns ranked, filtered, highlighted results in under 50ms. Pinecone returns vector IDs and scores — your app assembles the rest.

widget.title: Full search UX included
widget.description: AACsearch includes a hosted widget, analytics dashboard, and SDK. Pinecone is an API — all UX is your responsibility.

ecommerce.title: E-commerce search
ecommerce.description: AACsearch combines keyword and vector search for e-commerce. Pinecone is vector-only, missing BM25, typo tolerance, facets, and filtering for product search.

hybrid.title: Hybrid search
hybrid.description: AACsearch supports hybrid keyword + vector search out of the box. Pinecone requires a separate keyword search service alongside vector queries.

latency.title: Search latency
latency.description: AACsearch maintains sub-50ms latency for mixed keyword-vector queries. Pinecone vector-only queries can be fast but combining with keyword search adds overhead.

vsAlgoliaPage.title: AACsearch vs Algolia — 5x cheaper alternative
vsAlgoliaPage.description: Detailed comparison of AACsearch vs Algolia: pricing, multi-tenancy, performance, open-source Typesense engine, and migration guide. See why teams paying $200+/month on Algolia are switching to AACsearch for 5x savings.

vsAlgolia.title: AACsearch vs Algolia — Head-to-Head
vsAlgolia.subtitle: Algolia is the market leader with excellent DX. AACsearch is 5x cheaper, built on open-source Typesense, and adds native multi-tenancy with scoped tokens.

pricing.title: Pricing (5x cheaper)
pricing.description: Algolia charges per search operation and per record stored, with tiered minimums that surprise teams at scale. AACsearch charges per search-unit with no minimum beyond the free tier — typically 5x cheaper for 100K+ monthly searches.

performance.title: Performance
performance.description: Both deliver sub-50ms search. AACsearch uses an open-source Typesense engine with consistent p99 latency. Algolia's shared infrastructure can introduce latency spikes during peak traffic.

multiTenancy.title: Multi-tenancy
multiTenancy.description: Algolia uses per-index API keys with optional user tokens for scoping. AACsearch uses HMAC-scoped tokens with mandatory AND-combined filters — tenant isolation is architecturally enforced, not just policy.

security.title: Security
security.description: AACsearch adds per-key origin allow-lists, rate limiting, quota enforcement, and tenant isolation at every query layer. Algolia provides API key management with IP ranges and basic rate limiting. AACsearch covers what Algolia charges extra for.

migration.title: Migration
migration.description: Both APIs are structurally similar. AACsearch provides full Typesense data export and a Typesense-compatible API. Most teams complete the migration in hours using a re-index script. Algolia's proprietary format means vendor lock-in.

openSource.title: Open-source engine
openSource.description: AACsearch runs on Typesense, a fully open-source search engine (GPLv3). You can export your data at any time and run it yourself. Algolia uses a proprietary engine — no data portability if you leave.

ecosystem.title: Ecosystem & integrations
ecosystem.description: Algolia has a mature ecosystem — InstantSearch widgets, connectors, and extensive docs. AACsearch offers a UI widget (React, Vue, vanilla JS), real-time webhook connectors (PrestaShop, Bitrix, Shopify), and a REST API with Browser SDK.

verdict.title: When to choose AACsearch
verdict.description: If you spend $200+/month on Algolia, need native multi-tenancy with scoped tokens, want open-source portability, or prefer predictable flat pricing — AACsearch is the clear choice.

vsAlgolia.codeTitle: Swap the client, keep the query
vsAlgolia.codeSubtitle: Migration is hours, not weeks. AACsearch's Typesense-compatible API mirrors Algolia's query patterns.

decision.title: Decision matrix
decision.subtitle: Use this quick reference to decide which platform fits your use case best.

aacsearch.vendorFit: Teams scaling search, SaaS products, multi-tenant apps

decision.footnote: Costs are approximate and based on 100K monthly search operations and 10K records. Actual pricing varies by usage pattern.
